# Week 04 - Lecture 01

## Rough Agenda

### **4:05 pm - 5:55 pm** 

 + Linear model diagnostics
   - Recap residual plots (@sec-qq)
 +  Polynomial regression (@sec-poly)
    
### Before we begin **sort yourselves into groups** following @sec-groups

::: {.callout-tip}
## Straight(ish) lines
 
 + Critically evaluate linear model fit
 + Include polynomial terms in a linear model
 + Fit, draw inference from and critique polynomial regression models
:::




## Linear model diagnostics

<center><img src="https://github.com/statbiscuit/swots/blob/master/figs_n_gifs/lm.gif?raw=true" alt="GIF of linear model residuals"></center>

Otherwise known as *least squares* regression, by default `lm()` seeks to minimize the squared Euclidean distance between the observations and the fitted line.



### Residual plots (*a recap*) {#sec-qq}

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Blank Residual Plots
<center><img src="runsheets/img/blank_diagnostic.png" height="700px" alt="blank lm diagnostic plots"></center>
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## QQ plot cheatsheet `lm()`
<center><img src="runsheets/img/qq_residuals.png" height="700px" alt="blank lm diagnostic plots"></center>
:::


::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Outliers... (*Charlotte's bugbear*)

<center><img src="runsheets/img/outlawing_outliers.png" height="700px" alt="blank lm diagnostic plots"></center>
:::



### Light response {#sec-light}

Below, we investigate the relationship between the photosynthetic rate (µmol CO2 $m^-2 s^-1$) of sunflower leaves and light intensity (`PAR`, µmol $m^-2 s^-1$) (the plot below shows a subset of the data detailed in @lightresponse).


```{r, eval = TRUE, echo = FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
require(ggplot2)
require(photosynthesisLRC) 
data <- sunflowers[sunflowers$Replicate == 1,]
ggplot(data, aes(x = PARi, y = A)) +
  geom_point() + theme_light() +
  xlab("PAR (µmol m^-2 s^-1)") + ylab("Photosynthetic rate (µmol CO2 m^-2 s^-1)")
```



What does a linear model look like for these data?

```{r, echo = FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
fit <- lm(A ~ PARi, data = data)
ggplot(data, aes(x = PARi, y = A)) +
  geom_point(col = "grey") + theme_light() +
  xlab("PAR (µmol m^-2 s^-1)") + ylab("Photosynthetic rate (µmol CO2 m^-2 s^-1)") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ x, se = FALSE, col = "black", size = 2) + 
  ggtitle("Fitted linear model: Photosynthetic rate ~ PAR")
```

And the residual plots?

```{r, echo = FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
gglm::gglm(fit)
```

OK, so not a great fit. Could we perhaps improve our fit using other variables? Note, these data are contained in the `R` package `photosynthesisLRC` (@photosynthesisLRC) as the object `sunflowers`:

```{r, eval = FALSE}
require(photosynthesisLRC)
data <- sunflowers[sunflowers$Replicate == 1,]
```

In your groups, fit, interpret, and critique the models below? Which is the "best" fitting model (out of the three fitted below)? Create a plot of this model and be prepared to share it as I wander around the class.

```{r, eval = FALSE}
## MODEL 1
fit_01 <- lm(A ~ PARi, data = data) 
summary(fit_01)
anova(fit_01)
## MODEL 2
fit_02 <- lm(A ~ PARi + Species, data = data)
summary(fit_02)
anova(fit_02)
## MODEL 3
fit_03 <- lm(A ~ PARi * Species, data = data)
summary(fit_03)
anova(fit_03)
## model fit
AIC(fit_01, fit_02, fit_03)
anova(fit_01,fit_02, fit_03)
```


::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Model comparison quiz

<style>
.quiz-option {
  padding: 6px;
  border: 1px solid #ccc;
  margin: 4px 0;
  cursor: pointer;
  border-radius: 5px;
}
.correct { background-color: #c8f7c5; }
.incorrect { background-color: #f7c5c5; }
</style>

<script>
function checkAnswer(el, correct) {
  const options = el.parentElement.querySelectorAll('.quiz-option');
  options.forEach(opt => opt.classList.remove('correct', 'incorrect'));
  el.classList.add(correct ? 'correct' : 'incorrect');
}
</script>

---

**Q1. Which model is preferred based on AIC?**

<div>
  <div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">Model 1</div>
  <div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">Model 2</div>
  <div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,true)">Model 3</div>
  <div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">All models are equivalent</div>
</div>

---

**Q2. What does the ANOVA comparison between Model 1 and Model 2 indicate?**

<div>
  <div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">PARi is not important</div>
  <div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,true)">Adding Species improves model fit</div>
  <div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">Model 1 is better</div>
  <div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">There is no difference between models</div>
</div>

---

**Q3. What does the ANOVA comparison between Model 2 and Model 3 indicate?**

<div>
  <div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">The interaction is not needed</div>
  <div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,true)">The interaction between PARi and Species improves model fit</div>
  <div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">Model 2 is preferred</div>
  <div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">Species has no effect</div>
</div>

---

**Q4. Which statement best describes Model 3 biologically?**

<div>
  <div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">All species have the same relationship with light</div>
  <div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">Species only differ in intercepts</div>
  <div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,true)">Species differ in both baseline photosynthesis and response to light</div>
  <div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">Light has no effect</div>
</div>

---

**Q5. Why is Model 3 preferred despite having more parameters?**

<div>
  <div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">More parameters always make a model better</div>
  <div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,true)">It sufficiently improves fit to justify the added complexity</div>
  <div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">Because it has the largest R-squared</div>
  <div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">Because ANOVA always selects more complex models</div>
</div>
:::

## Polynomial regression  {#sec-poly}


Now, @photosynthesisLRC is an `R` package that 

> Provides functions for modeling, comparing, and visualizing photosynthetic light response curves using established mechanistic and empirical models like the rectangular hyperbola Michaelis-Menton based models...

For example, one such function is Equation 1 of @lobo2013lightresponse:

$$A = \frac{\alpha \, I \, A_{\max}}{\alpha \, I + A_{\max}} - R_d$$

where, $A$ = net photosynthesis, $I$ = irradiance (light intensity), $\alpha$ = apparent quantum efficiency (initial slope), $A_{\max}$ = maximum gross photosynthesis, and $R_d$ = dark respiration.

Below we plot this mechanistic non-linear model for our data in `R`.

```{r}
## plotting a mechanistic (non-linear) model to our data
photosyn_fun <- function(PARi, alpha = 5.4e-02, Amax = 27.5, Rd = 1.94) {
  (alpha * PARi * Amax) / (alpha * PARi + Amax) - Rd
}
pari <- seq(min(data$PARi), max(data$PARi), length.out = 1000)
preds <- data.frame( A_expected = photosyn_fun(pari), PARi = pari)
ggplot(data, aes(x = PARi, y = A)) +
  geom_point(col = "grey") + theme_light() +
  geom_line(data = preds, aes(y = A_expected), size = 2) +
  xlab("PAR (µmol m^-2 s^-1)") + ylab("Photosynthetic rate (µmol CO2 m^-2 s^-1)") +
  ggtitle("Mechanistic model for the photosynthetic rate")
```


### Can we use `lm()` for this?

::: {.callout-important}
## Note: Statistical modelling and mechanical modelling are typically used for different purposes...

... but the aim of today is to show you how a polynomial regression model fitted using `lm()` can recreate the *shape* of a non-linear model
:::
 

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Polynomial regression

![](runsheets/img/poly_vs_I.png)
:::

```{r, echo = FALSE, messgae = FALSE}
poly_lm <- lm(A ~ poly(PARi, 4), data = data, na.action = na.fail)
## MuMIn::dredge(poly_lm)
ggplot(data, aes(x = PARi, y = A)) +
  geom_point(col = "grey") + theme_light() +
  xlab("PAR (µmol m^-2 s^-1)") + ylab("Photosynthetic rate (µmol CO2 m^-2 s^-1)") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ poly(x, 4), se = FALSE, col = "red", lty = "dashed", size = 2) + 
  geom_line(data = preds, aes(y = A_expected), col = "black", size = 1.5) +
  ggtitle("Fitted ploynomial linear model (red) vs Mechanistic model (black)")
```

Above show *my* best `lm()` model to 'recreate' the mechanistic curve above. Note

 1. I have used polynomial terms (e.g., terms like `I(x^a)` or `poly(x, a)`, I'm not telling you which ones!)
 2. I have not included `Species` information, but from above it seems like this would be important information to include in a model...


**In your groups discuss what may be an appropriate model for these data. This will be an iterative process. Fit & interpret your "best" model, Discuss, how you decided on your "best" model.**

You may find the function `dredge` from the `R` package `MuMIn` useful for model selection. `dredge()` generates a model selection table of models with combinations of fixed effect terms in the given global (full) model. By default `dredge()` uses AICc as its model comparison metric. (p.s. you may need to set  `na.action = na.fail` in your `lm()` call for `dregde()` to work)



:::{.callout-warning icon=false}
## <i class="fa fa-question-circle" aria-hidden="true"></i> <i class="fa fa-users" aria-hidden="true"></i> <i class="fa fa-pencil-square" aria-hidden="true"></i> What's your best polynomial?

Once you've decided on your 'best fitting' model open [StatChat](https://canvas.auckland.ac.nz/courses/141056/external_tools/38296) and **individually** write out your model formula. We'll then compare the class' models!
:::
